---
title: "Global Temperature Anomalies in Practice"
subtitle: "An Open, Reproducible Framework for Baseline Harmonization and ENSO-Aware Visualization"
date: today
author:
  - name: J. Eduardo Vera-Valdés
    email: eduardo@math.aau.dk
    url: https://everval.github.io
    affiliations:
      - name: Aalborg University
        department: Department of Mathematical Sciences
        country: Denmark
      - name: CoRE
        department: "Center for Research in Energy: Economics and Markets"
        country: Denmark
    orcid: 0000-0002-0337-8055
    corresponding: true
abstract: |
  This paper develops a processing pipeline to download and process global temperature anomaly data from multiple datasets. The workflow downloads and compiles five of the most widely used global temperature anomaly datasets: HadCRUT5, GISTEMP, NOAAGlobalTemp, Berkeley Earth, and ERA5. The data is processed to calculate the temperature anomalies relative to the 1850-1900 baseline, the preindustrial level, making it easy to compare the datasets. The paper also plots the data and highlight El Niño and La Niña events using the ONI dataset. This makes is possible to visually inspect the relationship between global temperature anomalies and El Niño and La Niña events. The compiled dataset is saved in a CSV file and is directly accessible via the paper's repository.
keywords: [Global Temperature Anomalies, Preindustrial Baseline, El Niño Southern Oscillation]
execute:
  enabled: false
  engine: julia
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
bibliography: library.bib
---

## Introduction

Global temperature anomalies are central to climate variability and change analysis, but practical use across datasets is often limited by inconsistent baseline periods and heterogeneous data formats. These differences reduce comparability, increase processing effort, and weaken reproducibility in applied climate research and services.

@tbl-baselines summarizes the baseline periods, formats, and key notes for five of the most widely used global temperature anomaly datasets: HadCRUT5 [@HadCRUT5], GISTEMP [@GISTEMP], NOAAGlobalTemp [@NOAAGlobalTemp], Berkeley Earth [@BerkeleyEarth], and ERA5 [@ERA5]. The table highlights the operational challenges practitioners face when working with these datasets, including the need for custom processing to collect and harmonize data, and the lack of a common preindustrial baseline that is critical for climate change analysis.

| Dataset        | Baseline Period                    | Format      | Notes |
|----------|------------------|---------------|-----------------------------------|
| HadCRUT5       | 1961–1990  | CSV           | Values are released later than other datasets. |
| GISTEMP        | 1951–1980  | CSV           | Data start in 1880, so pre‑industrial anomalies need an extra adjustment. |
| NOAAGlobalTemp | 1991–2020  | ASCII         | Filenames are versioned so the URL changes with each update. |
| Berkeley Earth | 1951–1980  | TXT           | Space-delimited files. |
| ERA5           | No fixed baseline       | NetCDF  | No global average is reported and users must compute anomalies relative to a chosen climatology themselves. |
: Note: Baseline periods are based on dataset documentation and may be updated by data providers. Always check the latest documentation for each dataset. {#tbl-baselines    }

This paper addresses that operational gap by providing a concise, reproducible setup that harmonizes major global temperature anomaly datasets to a common preindustrial baseline (1850-1900) and a common tabular structure. The workflow is designed for direct reuse, supporting Open Data and Open Science while improving usability for climate services and practitioner workflows. The codebase is implemented in Julia [@Julia] to combine high readability with fast execution.

We compile and standardize data from the datasets listed in @tbl-baselines, calculating anomalies relative to the 1850-1900 baseline to facilitate direct comparison across datasets. This allows users to easily compare temperature anomalies across datasets without needing to perform their own baseline adjustments. The workflow also includes data processing steps to handle different formats and structures, ensuring that the final compiled dataset is consistent and ready for analysis. Moreover, to strengthen interpretation, we integrate the Oceanic Nino Index [@SST_Part1; @SST_Part2] and annotate El Nino and La Nina phases in the visualizations, making short-term ENSO variability easier to interpret alongside long-term warming.

The remainder of the notebook is organized as follows: we load the required packages and helper functions, process each dataset, construct a unified dataset, and present comparative plots with ENSO annotations. The compiled dataset is saved as a CSV file in the repository. The codebase is structured for reproducibility and reuse through clear documentation and modular functions that can be adapted to other datasets or baseline definitions.

## Necessary Packages

To ensure reproducibility and ease of use, this notebook uses a Julia project environment with pinned dependencies. The environment is defined by `Project.toml` (and `Manifest.toml` when present), allowing others to reproduce the same setup by activation. In practice, Julia's environment system makes reproducibility straightforward, while Python workflows can be less consistent when dependencies are not pinned.

```julia
# Initialize the project environment 

using Pkg
Pkg.activate(pwd())
```
The code above activates the project environment in the current directory. We then load the packages used for data download, processing, and visualization: `Dates`, `CSV`, `DataFrames`, `HTTP`, `CDSAPI`, `Statistics`, `NCDatasets`, and `Plots`. `Dates` and `Statistics` are included in the Julia standard library; the remaining packages can be installed with `Pkg.add`:

```julia
# Install necessary packages

Pkg.add(["Plots", "CSV", "DataFrames", "HTTP", "CDSAPI", "NCDatasets"])
```

After installation, packages are loaded with `using`. 

```julia
# Load the necessary packages

using Statistics, Dates, CSV, DataFrames, HTTP, CDSAPI, NCDatasets, Plots
```

Once the necessary packages are defined, we can proceed to download and process each dataset.

## HadCRUT5 {#sec-HadCRUT5}

The HadCRUT5 dataset is a global monthly average temperature dataset compiled by the Met Office Hadley Centre and the Climatic Research Unit at the University of East Anglia [@HadCRUT5]. It is one of the most widely used datasets for global temperature anomalies. The HadCRUT5 dataset is available in CSV format from the [Met Office website](https://www.metoffice.gov.uk/hadobs/hadcrut5/). The code below downloads the data, processes it, and saves it in a CSV file. 
```julia
# Download the HadCRUT temperature data 
# URL of the HadCRUT5 global monthly average CSV
hurl = "https://www.metoffice.gov.uk/hadobs/hadcrut5/data/HadCRUT.5.1.0.0/analysis/diagnostics/HadCRUT.5.1.0.0.analysis.summary_series.global.monthly.csv"
# Local filename to save
hfilename = "data/HadCRUT5_global_monthly_average.csv"
open(hfilename, "w") do io
    write(io, HTTP.get(hurl).body)
end
```

The next code snippet reads the downloaded CSV file, renames the columns for clarity, and extracts the date and raw temperature anomalies. The code then calculates the temperature anomalies relative to the 1850-1900 baseline and saves the processed data in a new CSV file. The last line shows the last 5 rows of the processed HadCRUT5 data.

```julia
rawhadcrut = CSV.read(hfilename, DataFrame)
rename!(rawhadcrut, :Time => :Date)
rename!(rawhadcrut, :"Anomaly (deg C)" => :RawTemperature)

hadcrut = rawhadcrut[!, [:Date, :RawTemperature]]

oldbase = mean(hadcrut[(hadcrut.Date.>=Date(1850, 1, 1)).&(hadcrut.Date.<Date(1900, 1, 1)), :RawTemperature])
hadcrut[!, :Temp] = hadcrut[!, :RawTemperature] .- oldbase;

CSV.write(hfilename, hadcrut)

last(hadcrut, 5) # Show the first 5 rows of the HadCRUT5 data
```

The HadCRUT5 dataset is now saved here [`HadCRUT5_global_monthly_average.csv`](data/HadCRUT5_global_monthly_average.csv). The data contains the date, raw temperature anomalies, and the temperature anomalies relative to the 1850-1900 baseline.

## NOAAGlobalTemp {#sec-NOAAGlobalTemp}

The NOAAGlobalTemp dataset is a global monthly average temperature dataset compiled by the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) [@NOAAGlobalTemp]. It is available in ASCII format from the [NOAA NCEI website](https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/land-based-station/noaa-global-temp), where space-delimited files are used. Moreover, the URL of the dataset depends on the month of the data (note the `...YYYYMM.asc` name). The code below downloads the latest data, processes it, and saves it in a CSV file. 

```julia
# Download the NOAAGlobalTemp temperature data 
# URL of the NOAAGlobalTemp global monthly average CSV ---yearmonth.asc
nurl = "https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/data/noaa-global-surface-temperature/v6.1/access/timeseries/aravg.mon.land_ocean.90S.90N.v6.1.0.202603.asc"
# Local filename to save
nfilename = "data/NOAA_global_monthly_average.csv"

# Download the file
open(nfilename, "w") do io
    write(io, HTTP.get(nurl).body)
end

lines = readlines(nfilename)
cleaned_lines = [join(split(strip(line)), ",") for line in lines]

# Write to file
write(nfilename, join(cleaned_lines, "\n"))
```

The code includes a step to clean the data and convert it to CSV format. Step-by-step, the `cleaned_lines` variable is created by reading each line of the downloaded file, stripping whitespace (`strip()`), splitting the line into components based on spaces (`split()`), and then joining those components with commas to create a CSV format (`join()`). The cleaned lines are then written back to the same file, effectively converting the original space-delimited ASCII file into a comma-separated CSV file.`

The data is then used to calculate the temperature anomalies relative to the 1850-1900 baseline, similar to the HadCRUT5 dataset. The last line shows the last 5 rows of the processed NOAAGlobalTemp data.

```julia
# Read the cleaned CSV file

rawnoaa = CSV.read(nfilename, DataFrame; delim=',', header=0)

fechas = Date.(rawnoaa.Column1, rawnoaa.Column2, 1) # Create Date column from Column1 and Column2

noaa = DataFrame(:Date=>fechas, :RawTemp=>rawnoaa.Column3)

oldbase = mean(noaa[(noaa.Date.>=Date(1850, 1, 1)).&(noaa.Date.<Date(1900, 1, 1)), :RawTemp])
noaa[!, :Temp] = noaa[!, :RawTemp] .- oldbase

CSV.write(nfilename, noaa)

last(noaa, 5) # Show the first 5 rows of the NOAAGlobalTemp data
```

The NOAAGlobalTemp dataset is now saved here [`NOAA_global_monthly_average.csv`](data/NOAA_global_monthly_average.csv). The data contains the date, raw temperature anomalies, and the temperature anomalies relative to the 1850-1900 baseline.


## GISTEMP {#sec-GISTEMP}

The GISTEMP dataset is a global monthly average temperature dataset [@GISTEMP]. It is available in CSV format from [the NASA GISS website](https://data.giss.nasa.gov/gistemp/). The data is provided in a wide format, where each row corresponds to one year and monthly values are stored in separate columns. To process this data, we need to convert it to a long format, where each row corresponds to a single month. The `longseries` function defined below performs this conversion.

```julia
# Function to convert wide format data to long format

function longseries(data)
    height = size(data, 1) # Number of rows, equivalent to the number of years
    last_row = 12 - count(ismissing, data[end, 2:13]) # Number of non-missing months in the last year

    many = (height - 1) * 12 + last_row # Total number of months in the long format
    long = zeros(many, 1) # Long format array

    for ii = 1:(height-1) # Loop through all years except the last one
        for jj = 1:12 # Loop through all months
            long[(ii-1)*12+jj] = data[ii, jj+1]
        end
    end

    for jj = 1:last_row # Loop through the last year
        long[(height-1)*12+jj] = data[height, jj+1]
    end

    return long
end
```

The `longseries` function takes a DataFrame in wide format and returns a long-format array. It calculates the total number of months based on the number of years, same as the number of rows, and the number of non-missing months in the last year. It then fills the long-format array by looping through the years and months.

Once the `longseries` function is defined, we can use it to process the GISTEMP data. The code below downloads the data, processes it, and saves it in a CSV file. 

```julia
# Download the GISTEMP temperature data 
# URL of the GISTEMP global monthly average CSV
gurl = "https://data.giss.nasa.gov/gistemp/tabledata_v4/GLB.Ts%2BdSST.csv"
# Local filename to save
gfilename = "data/GISTEMP_global_monthly_average.csv"
# Download the file

open(gfilename, "w") do io
    write(io, HTTP.get(gurl).body)
end
```

The data is then cleaned from the `***` string used to denote missing values, and converts it to long format using the `longseries` function.

```julia
# Removing missing values and converting to long format

longgistemp = CSV.read(gfilename, DataFrame, header=2, missingstring=["***"])
gistemp = longseries(longgistemp)[:]
```

A date array is created based on the number of months in the dataset, starting from January 1880. The raw temperature anomalies are then adjusted to calculate the temperature anomalies relative to the 1850-1900 baseline. Following the data source recommendation, the data is adjusted to a 1880-1900 baseine and then make a small adjustment of 0.038°C to account for the pre-1880 data^[https://data.giss.nasa.gov/gistemp/faq/#q102a].

```julia
Tt = length(gistemp) - 1

start = Date(1880, 1, 1) # Start date of the dataset
fin = start + Month(Tt) # End date of the dataset
fechas = collect(start:Month(1):fin) # Create a Date array

gistemp = DataFrame(:Date=>fechas, :RawTemp=>gistemp)

oldbase = mean(gistemp[(gistemp.Date.>=Date(1880, 1, 1)).&(gistemp.Date.<Date(1900, 1, 1)), :RawTemp])
gistemp[!, :Temp] = gistemp[!, :RawTemp] .- oldbase .+ 0.038 # Adjust for pre-1880 data

CSV.write(gfilename, gistemp)

last(gistemp, 5) # Show the first 5 rows of the GISTEMP data
```

The GISTEMP dataset is now saved here [`GISTEMP_global_monthly_average.csv`](data/GISTEMP_global_monthly_average.csv). The data contains the date, raw temperature anomalies, and the temperature anomalies relative to the 1850-1900 baseline.


## Berkeley Earth {#sec-BerkeleyEarth}

The Berkeley Earth dataset is a global monthly average temperature dataset [@BerkeleyEarth]. It is available in TXT format from [the Berkeley Earth website](https://berkeleyearth.org/) using space-delimited files. The code below downloads the data, processes it, and saves it in a CSV file.
```julia
# Download the Berkeley Earth temperature data
# URL of the Berkeley Earth global monthly average CSV
burl = "https://storage.googleapis.com/berkeley-earth-temperature-hr/global/Global_TAVG_monthly.txt"
# Local filename to save
bfilename = "data/BerkeleyEarth_global_monthly_average.csv"
# Download the file
open(bfilename, "w") do io
    write(io, HTTP.get(burl).body)
end
```

A new variable is created to store the date, which is constructed from the year and month columns in the dataset. The raw temperature anomalies are then adjusted to calculate the temperature anomalies relative to the 1850-1900 baseline. The processed data is saved in a new CSV file. The last line shows the last 5 rows of the processed Berkeley Earth data.

```julia
rawtemp = CSV.read(bfilename, DataFrame, comment="%", delim=" ", ignorerepeated=true)

colnames = [:Year, :Month, :Anomaly_Monthly, :Unc_Monthly,
            :Anomaly_Annual, :Unc_Annual, :Anomaly_5yr, :Unc_5yr,
            :Anomaly_10yr, :Unc_10yr, :Anomaly_20yr, :Unc_20yr]
rename!(rawtemp, colnames)

rawtemp.Date = Date.(rawtemp.Year, rawtemp.Month, 1) # Create Date column from Year and Month
rename!(rawtemp, :Anomaly_Monthly => :RawTemperature)

berkeley = rawtemp[!, [:Date, :RawTemperature]]

oldbase = mean(rawtemp[(rawtemp.Date.>=Date(1850, 1, 1)).&(rawtemp.Date.<Date(1900, 1, 1)), :RawTemperature])
rawtemp[!, :Temp] = rawtemp[!, :RawTemperature] .- oldbase

berkeley.Temp = rawtemp.Temp

CSV.write(bfilename, berkeley)

last(berkeley, 5) # Show the first 5 rows of the Berkeley Earth data
```

The Berkeley Earth dataset is now saved here [`BerkeleyEarth_global_monthly_average.csv`](data/BerkeleyEarth_global_monthly_average.csv). The data contains the date, raw temperature anomalies, and the temperature anomalies relative to the 1850-1900 baseline.

## ERA5 {#sec-ERA5}

ERA5 is the fifth generation ECMWF reanalysis for the global climate and weather [@ERA5]. Data is available from 1940 onwards in NetCDF format from the [Copernicus Climate Data Store](https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/datasets/reanalysis-era5-single-levels-monthly-means?tab=overview). The code to download and process the ERA5 data is shown below. 

Note that the API requires setting up a personal key in a `.cdsapirc` file, which should be placed in the root directory of the project. You can get a key by requesting it at the [Copernicus Climate Data Store website](https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/). The code below assumes that you have already set up the `.cdsapirc` file with your key. The `CDSAPI` package is used to interact with the Copernicus Climate Data Store API and download the data.

 The code below uses the `CDSAPI` package to download the data in a NetCDF file. The data is large, close to 1.5 GB, so it may take some time to download. To obtain the full dataset, the whole period from 1940 to 2026 is requested, but for update purposes, the code can be modified to request a smaller period, for example, from 2020 to present. The code also specifies the variable to download (2m temperature), the time resolution (monthly), and the data format (NetCDF). 
 
 The downloaded data is saved in the `data` directory with the name `era5_2m_temperature.nc`.


```julia
# Download the ERA5 temperature data
dataset = "reanalysis-era5-single-levels-monthly-means"

request = """{
    "product_type": ["monthly_averaged_reanalysis"],
    "variable": ["2m_temperature"],
    "year": [
        "1940", "1941", "1942", "1943", "1944", "1945",
        "1946", "1947", "1948", "1949", "1950", "1951",
        "1952", "1953", "1954", "1955", "1956", "1957",
        "1958", "1959", "1960", "1961", "1962", "1963",
        "1964", "1965", "1966", "1967", "1968", "1969",
        "1970", "1971", "1972", "1973", "1974", "1975",
        "1976", "1977", "1978", "1979", "1980", "1981",
        "1982", "1983", "1984", "1985", "1986", "1987",
        "1988", "1989", "1990", "1991", "1992", "1993",
        "1994", "1995", "1996", "1997", "1998", "1999",
        "2000", "2001", "2002", "2003", "2004", "2005",
        "2006", "2007", "2008", "2009", "2010", "2011",
        "2012", "2013", "2014", "2015", "2016", "2017",
        "2018", "2019", "2020", "2021", "2022", "2023", 
        "2024", "2025", "2026"
    ],
    "month": [
        "01", "02", "03",
        "04", "05", "06",
        "07", "08", "09",
        "10", "11", "12"
    ],
    "time": ["00:00"],
    "data_format": "netcdf",
    "download_format": "unarchived"
}"""

CDSAPI.retrieve(dataset, request, "data/era5_2m_temperature_2020.nc")
```

Once the data is downloaded, we can use the `NCDatasets` package to read the NetCDF file and process the data. The code below reads the data, extracts the latitude, longitude, time, and temperature variables, and calculates the global average temperature anomaly for each month.

```julia
filename = "data/era5_2m_temperature.nc";
ds = NCDataset(filename, "r");

lat_era = ds["latitude"][:]
lon_era = ds["longitude"][:]
time_era = ds["valid_time"][:]
date_era = Date.(time_era)
lat = lat_era * π/180
```

Note that the latitude values are converted from degrees to radians for the calculation of the global average temperature anomaly, which involves weighting the temperature values by the cosine of the latitude. Basically, grids closer to the poles represent smaller areas than grids near the equator, so they are weighted less in the global average calculation. The cosine of the latitude is used as a weighting factor to account for this difference in area representation. Furthermore, the temperature values in the ERA5 dataset are in Kelvin, so they will need to be converted to Celsius by subtracting 273.15 from the global mean temperature values.

Processing the data is computationally intensive, so we will process the data in chunks of 120 months (10 years) to avoid memory issues. The code below calculates the global average temperature anomaly for each month and saves it in a CSV file. 

```julia
ini = 1
chunk_size = 120 # Number of months to skip for plotting

era5_chunk = DataFrame()
global_mean_C = zeros(length(date_era))

while ini < length(date_era)

for i in ini:(ini+chunk_size-1)
    t2m = ds["t2m"][:, :, i]  # 2D slice [lon, lat] for first time; in K
    t_lon_mean = mean(t2m, dims=1)  # [lat]; drops new dim=2
    weights = cos.(lat)
    global_mean_K = sum(t_lon_mean' .* weights) / sum(weights)
    global_mean_C[i] = global_mean_K - 273.15
end

ini = ini + chunk_size

end
```

The `global_mean_C` variable now contains the global average temperature anomaly for each month in degrees Celsius. The code below displays the global mean temperature anomalies and saves the data in a CSV file.

```julia
display(global_mean_C)

era5_raw = DataFrame(Date=date_era, RawTemperature=global_mean_C)

CSV.write("data/ERA5_global_monthly_average_raw.csv", era5_raw)
```

Once the monthly global mean temperature anomalies are calculated, we can calculate the temperature anomalies relative to the 1850-1900 baseline. Unlike the other datasets, ERA5 does not provide a fixed baseline period, so we need to calculate the climatology for the period 1991-2020 and then calculate the anomalies relative to this climatology. The code below performs these calculations and saves the processed data in a new CSV file. Finally, we add the offset to convert to preindustrial anomalies and save the data in a CSV file.

```julia
time_dates = Date.(ds["valid_time"][:])  # If not already
start_1991 = findfirst(≥(Date(1991,1,1)), time_dates)
end_2020 = findfirst(≥(Date(2021,1,1)), time_dates) - 1
n_clim = end_2020 - start_1991 + 1  # ~360

month_clim = zeros(12)
for m in 1:12
    idx = (month.(time_dates[start_1991:end_2020]) .== m)
    month_clim[m] = mean(global_mean_C[start_1991:end_2020][idx])
end

anom_1991_2020 = global_mean_C .- month_clim[month.(time_dates)]
offset_preind = 0.88  # Latest ERA5-to-1850-1900; check HadCRUT5 [web:73]
pa_anom = anom_1991_2020 .+ offset_preind

println("2025 annual PA anomaly: ", round(mean(pa_anom[end-11:end]), digits=2), "°C")

era5 = DataFrame(Date=time_dates, RawTemperature=anom_1991_2020, Temp=pa_anom)
CSV.write("data/ERA5_global_monthly_average.csv", era5)
```

The snippet below reads the processed ERA5 data from the CSV file and shows the last 5 rows of the data.

```julia
#| output: false
#| echo: false
erafilename = "data/ERA5_global_monthly_average.csv"
era5 = CSV.read(erafilename, DataFrame)

last(era5, 5) # Show the first 5 rows of the ERA5 data
```

## Oceanic Niño Index (ONI) {#sec-ONI}

El Niño (La Niña) is a phenomenon in the equatorial Pacific Ocean characterized by a five consecutive 3-month running mean of sea surface temperature anomalies in the Niño 3.4 region that is above (below) the threshold of +0.5°C (-0.5°C). 

The data is obtained from the Extended Reconstructed Sea Surface Temperature (ERSST) dataset, which is a global monthly analysis of sea surface temperature data derived from the International Comprehensive Ocean–Atmosphere Dataset (ICOADS) [@SST_Part1; @SST_Part2]. The ONI data is available in CSV format from [the NOAA Climate Monitoring website](https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/access/monitoring/enso/). The code below downloads the data, processes it, and saves it in a CSV file.

```julia
# Download the ONI data
ourl = "https://www.cpc.ncep.noaa.gov/data/indices/sstoi.indices"
ofilename = "data/Nino_data.csv"

open(ofilename, "w") do io
    write(io, HTTP.get(ourl).body)
end

lines = readlines(ofilename)
cleaned_lines = [join(split(strip(line)), ",") for line in lines]

# Write to file
write(ofilename, join(cleaned_lines, "\n"))

rawoni = CSV.read(ofilename, DataFrame; delim=',', header=1)
fechas = Date.(rawoni.YR, rawoni.MON, 1) # Create Date column from YR and MON

oni = DataFrame(Date=fechas, Anom=rawoni[!, :ANOM_3])

CSV.write(ofilename, oni)

last(oni, 5) # Show the first 5 rows of the ONI data
```

The ONI dataset is now saved here [`Nino_data.csv`](data/Nino_data.csv). The data contains the date and the ONI anomalies.

## Merge all datasets {#sec-merge-all-datasets}

The code below merges all the datasets into a single dataset. It uses the `leftjoin` function to merge the datasets on the `Date` column. The resulting dataset contains the temperature anomalies for each dataset, as well as the ONI data. The merged dataset is saved in a CSV file.

```julia
# Load the datasets
hfilename = "data/HadCRUT5_global_monthly_average.csv"
gfilename = "data/GISTEMP_global_monthly_average.csv"
nfilename = "data/NOAA_global_monthly_average.csv"
bfilename = "data/BerkeleyEarth_global_monthly_average.csv"
erafilename = "data/ERA5_global_monthly_average.csv"
ofilename = "data/Nino_data.csv"

hadcrut = CSV.read(hfilename, DataFrame)
gistemp = CSV.read(gfilename, DataFrame)
noaa = CSV.read(nfilename, DataFrame)
berkeley = CSV.read(bfilename, DataFrame)
era5 = CSV.read(erafilename, DataFrame)
oni = CSV.read(ofilename, DataFrame)

# Dates
min_date = minimum([minimum(hadcrut.Date), minimum(gistemp.Date), minimum(noaa.Date), minimum(berkeley.Date), minimum(oni.Date)])
max_date = maximum([maximum(hadcrut.Date), maximum(gistemp.Date), maximum(noaa.Date), maximum(berkeley.Date), maximum(oni.Date)])
complete_dates = collect(min_date:Month(1):max_date)
compiled_data = DataFrame(Date=complete_dates)

# HadCRUT5
compiled_data = leftjoin(compiled_data, hadcrut, on = :Date)
rename!(compiled_data, :RawTemperature => :HadCRUT_RawTemperature)
rename!(compiled_data, :Temp => :HadCRUT_Temp)
sort!(compiled_data, :Date)

# GISTEMP
compiled_data = leftjoin(compiled_data, gistemp, on = :Date)
rename!(compiled_data, :RawTemp => :GISTEMP_RawTemperature)
rename!(compiled_data, :Temp => :GISTEMP_Temp)  
sort!(compiled_data, :Date)

# NOAA
compiled_data = leftjoin(compiled_data, noaa, on = :Date)
rename!(compiled_data, :RawTemp => :NOAA_RawTemperature)
rename!(compiled_data, :Temp => :NOAA_Temp)
sort!(compiled_data, :Date)

# Berkeley Earth
compiled_data = leftjoin(compiled_data, berkeley, on = :Date)
rename!(compiled_data, :RawTemperature => :Berkeley_RawTemperature)
rename!(compiled_data, :Temp => :Berkeley_Temp)
sort!(compiled_data, :Date)

# ERA5
compiled_data = leftjoin(compiled_data, era5, on = :Date)
rename!(compiled_data, :RawTemperature => :ERA5_RawTemperature)
rename!(compiled_data, :Temp => :ERA5_Temp)
sort!(compiled_data, :Date)

# ONI
compiled_data = leftjoin(compiled_data, oni, on = :Date)
rename!(compiled_data, :Anom => :ONI_Anomaly)   
sort!(compiled_data, :Date)

# Save the compiled data
compiled_filename = "data/Compiled_Global_Temperature_Data.csv"
CSV.write(compiled_filename, compiled_data)

last(compiled_data, 5) # Show the last 5 rows of the compiled data
```

The compiled dataset is now saved here [`Compiled_Global_Temperature_Data.csv`](data/Compiled_Global_Temperature_Data.csv). The data contains the date, raw temperature anomalies for each dataset, and the temperature anomalies relative to the 1850-1900 baseline. It also includes the ONI anomalies.

## Plot the data {#sec-plot-data}

Loading the compiled data and setting plot aesthetics.

```julia
#| warning: false
# Load the compiled data and plot packages

using Plots
compiled_data = CSV.read(compiled_filename, DataFrame)

# Set plot aesthetics
theme(:ggplot2)
default(
        fontfamily = "Computer Modern",
        tickfontsize = 10,        legendfontsize = 10,
        titlefontsize = 12,
        xlabelfontsize = 10,
        ylabelfontsize = 10,
        titlefontfamily = "Computer Modern",
        legendfontfamily = "Computer Modern",
        tickfontfamily = "Computer Modern"
)

# Extract the dates for x-axis ticks
# This will be used for the x-axis ticks in the plot
xls = compiled_data.Date;
```

Plotting the data.

```julia
# Plot the data one dataset at a time, for clarity
p = plot(compiled_data.Date, compiled_data.HadCRUT_Temp, label="HadCRUT5", xlabel="Date (monthly)", ylabel="Temperature Anomaly (°C)", title="Global Temperature Anomalies", linewidth=0.5, markershape=:circle, markersize=1)
plot!(compiled_data.Date, compiled_data.GISTEMP_Temp, label="GISTEMP", xlabel="Date (monthly)", ylabel="Temperature Anomaly (°C)", title="Global Temperature Anomalies" , linewidth=0.5, markershape=:diamond, markersize=1)
plot!(compiled_data.Date, compiled_data.NOAA_Temp, label="NOAAGlobalTemp", xlabel="Date (monthly)", ylabel="Temperature Anomaly (°C)", title="Global Temperature Anomalies" , linewidth=0.5, markershape=:+, markersize=1)
plot!(compiled_data.Date, compiled_data.Berkeley_Temp, label="Berkeley Earth", xlabel="Date (monthly)", ylabel="Temperature Anomaly (°C)", title="Global Temperature Anomalies", linewidth=0.5, markershape=:xcross, markersize=1)
plot!(compiled_data.Date, compiled_data.ERA5_Temp, label="ERA5", xlabel="Date (monthly)", ylabel="Temperature Anomaly (°C)", title="Global Temperature Anomalies", linewidth=0.5, markershape=:star, markersize=1)
plot!(legend=:topleft, xticks=(xls[1:180:end], Dates.format.(xls[1:180:end], "Y"))) # Set x-axis ticks every 180 months
display(p)
```

The plot shows the global temperature anomalies for each dataset. The HadCRUT5 dataset is shown in blue, GISTEMP in orange, NOAAGlobalTemp in green, Berkeley Earth in purple, and ERA5 in brown. The x-axis represents the date (monthly), and the y-axis represents the temperature anomaly in degrees Celsius.

```julia
#| output: false
# Save the plot

savefig("data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies.pdf")
savefig("data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies.png")
```

The plot is saved as a PDF file as [`Global_Temperature_Anomalies.pdf`](data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies.pdf).

### The last 30 years

Zooming in on the last 30 years of data.

```julia
# Zoom in on the last 30 years of data

compiled_data_zoomed = compiled_data[compiled_data.Date .>= Date(1995, 1, 1), :]

p_zoomed = plot(compiled_data_zoomed.Date, compiled_data_zoomed.HadCRUT_Temp, label="HadCRUT5", xlabel="Date (monthly)", ylabel="Temperature Anomaly (°C)", title="Global Temperature Anomalies (Last 30 Years)", linewidth=0.5, markershape=:circle, markersize=1)
plot!(compiled_data_zoomed.Date, compiled_data_zoomed.GISTEMP_Temp, label="GISTEMP", xlabel="Date (monthly)", ylabel="Temperature Anomaly (°C)", title="Global Temperature Anomalies (Last 30 Years)", linewidth=0.5, markershape=:diamond, markersize=1)
plot!(compiled_data_zoomed.Date, compiled_data_zoomed.NOAA_Temp, label="NOAAGlobalTemp", xlabel="Date (monthly)", ylabel="Temperature Anomaly (°C)", title="Global Temperature Anomalies (Last 30 Years)", linewidth=0.5, markershape=:+, markersize=1)
plot!(compiled_data_zoomed.Date, compiled_data_zoomed.Berkeley_Temp, label="Berkeley Earth", xlabel="Date (monthly)", ylabel="Temperature Anomaly (°C)", title="Global Temperature Anomalies (Last 30 Years)", linewidth=0.5, markershape=:xcross, markersize=1)
plot!(compiled_data_zoomed.Date, compiled_data_zoomed.ERA5_Temp, label="ERA5", xlabel="Date (monthly)", ylabel="Temperature Anomaly (°C)", title="Global Temperature Anomalies (Last 30 Years)", linewidth=0.5, markershape=:star, markersize=1)
plot!(legend=:topleft, xticks=(compiled_data_zoomed.Date[1:60:end], Dates.format.(compiled_data_zoomed.Date[1:60:end], "Y")))
display(p_zoomed)
```

The zoomed-in plot shows the global temperature anomalies for each dataset over the last 30 years. The HadCRUT5 dataset is shown in blue, GISTEMP in orange, NOAAGlobalTemp in green, Berkeley Earth in purple, and ERA5 in brown. The x-axis represents the date (monthly), and the y-axis represents the temperature anomaly in degrees Celsius.

```julia
#| output: false
# Save the zoomed plot

savefig("data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years.pdf")
savefig("data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years.png")
```

The plot is saved as a PDF file as [`Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years.pdf`](data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years.pdf).

### Adding El Niño and La Niña events {#sec-Adding-ONI}

To add the periods of El Niño and La Niña events to the plot, we will use the SST anomalies. An El Niño event is defined as a period when the SST anomaly is above +0.5°C, while a La Niña event is defined as a period when the SST anomaly is below -0.5°C. 

First, we need to classify the SST anomalies.

```julia
# Classify SST anomalies into El Niño, La Niña, and Neutral events

ONI_Anomaly = compiled_data_zoomed.ONI_Anomaly[.!ismissing.(compiled_data_zoomed.ONI_Anomaly)]

T = length(ONI_Anomaly)
indicator = zeros(Int, T)

for i in 1:T
    if ONI_Anomaly[i] >= 0.5
        indicator[i] = 1
    elseif ONI_Anomaly[i] <= -0.5
        indicator[i] = -1
    else
        indicator[i] = 0
    end
end
```

Then we can add the El Niño and La Niña events to the plot. The shaded areas will indicate the periods of El Niño (red) and La Niña (blue) events based on the SST anomalies.

```julia
p_zoomed_oni = p_zoomed

i = 1
while i <= length(indicator)
    current_val = indicator[i]
    if current_val in (-1, 1)
        start_idx = i
        while i <= length(indicator) && indicator[i] == current_val
            i = i + 1
        end
        stop_idx = i - 1
        if (stop_idx <= start_idx) || (stop_idx - start_idx < 4)
            continue
        else
            vspan!(p_zoomed_oni, [compiled_data_zoomed.Date[start_idx], compiled_data_zoomed.Date[stop_idx]], color=current_val == 1 ? :red : :blue, alpha=0.1, label ="")
        end
    else
        i = i + 1
    end
end

display(p_zoomed_oni)
```

The plot now shows the global temperature anomalies for each dataset over the last 30 years, with shaded areas indicating El Niño (red) and La Niña (blue) events based on the ONI anomalies. The x-axis represents the date (monthly), and the y-axis represents the temperature anomaly in degrees Celsius.

```julia
#| output: false

# Save the plot with ONI events

savefig("data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years_ONI.pdf")
savefig("data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years_ONI.png")
```

The plot is saved as a PDF file as [`Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years_ONI.pdf`](data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years_ONI.pdf).

## References 

::: {#refs}
:::

## Citation

If you use any of the data or code in this notebook, please cite the original datasets and this notebook as follows:

```bibtex
@article{vera-valdes2026,
  author = {Vera-Valdés, J. Eduardo},
  title = {Data visualization and comparison of global temperature anomalies from multiple datasets},
  journal = {Submitted},
  year = {2026},
}
```
